content.text: Pipeline Stages for GenAI Application Delivery

A GenAI application delivery pipeline shares the same structural backbone as any modern CI/CD pipeline — source trigger, build, validate, deploy, monitor — but each stage carries responsibilities that do not exist in conventional software delivery: non-deterministic output evaluation, prompt versioning, LLM-as-judge quality gates, and post-deploy drift detection.
This document walks each stage in sequence, explains what changes because of the LLM in the stack, and provides the configuration and code patterns needed to implement it reliably.
Stage 1: Source Trigger and Branch Policy

The pipeline triggers on a git push or pull request. For GenAI applications, branch policy must also version the artefacts that change model behaviour — prompts, retrieval configurations, and model version pins — alongside application code:

# .github/branch-protection.yml (enforced via GitHub API or Terraform)
# Require PR review for any change to:
#   - prompts/
#   - eval/
#   - config/model_versions.yaml
# These changes are treated as behaviour-changing and must pass the eval gate
# before merge — not just lint and unit tests.

# In CI, detect which artefact type changed:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  detect-changes:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      prompt_changed: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.prompts }}
      eval_changed: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.eval }}
      app_changed: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.app }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: dorny/paths-filter@v3
        id: filter
        with:
          filters: |
            prompts:
              - 'prompts/**'
            eval:
              - 'eval/**'
              - 'tests/eval/**'
            app:
              - 'app/**'
              - 'Dockerfile'
              - 'requirements.txt'
Stage 2: Build

The build stage produces a Docker image and captures the version envelope that ties together every piece of the deployed system. The version envelope is embedded in the image as a label and persisted as a build artifact:

build:
    needs: detect-changes
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image_digest: ${{ steps.push.outputs.digest }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Read version envelope
        id: versions
        run: |
          APP_VERSION=$(cat VERSION)
          PROMPT_VERSION=$(cat prompts/.version)
          MODEL=$(jq -r '.model' config/model_versions.json)
          echo "APP_VERSION=$APP_VERSION" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "PROMPT_VERSION=$PROMPT_VERSION" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "MODEL=$MODEL" >> $GITHUB_ENV

      - name: Build Docker image with version labels
        run: |
          docker build \
            --label "app.version=$APP_VERSION" \
            --label "prompt.version=$PROMPT_VERSION" \
            --label "llm.model=$MODEL" \
            --label "git.sha=$GITHUB_SHA" \
            -t myregistry/myapp:$APP_VERSION .

      - name: Generate SBOM
        run: |
          syft myregistry/myapp:$APP_VERSION -o spdx-json > sbom.json

      - name: Push and capture digest
        id: push
        run: |
          DIGEST=$(docker push myregistry/myapp:$APP_VERSION --quiet)
          echo "digest=$DIGEST" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Write version envelope artifact
        run: |
          jq -n --arg app "$APP_VERSION" --arg prompt "$PROMPT_VERSION" \
             --arg model "$MODEL" --arg sha "$GITHUB_SHA" \
             '{app_version:$app, prompt_version:$prompt, model:$model, git_sha:$sha}' \
             > version_envelope.json

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with: { name: version-envelope, path: version_envelope.json }
Stage 3: Validate
Conventional checks run first because they are fast (seconds to minutes) and eliminate the largest class of failures before the expensive eval stage:

validate:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }

      - name: Install
        run: pip install ruff mypy pytest coverage

      - name: Lint and type check
        run: |
          ruff check app/ eval/
          mypy app/ --ignore-missing-imports

      - name: Unit tests with coverage
        run: |
          coverage run -m pytest tests/unit/ -q
          coverage report --fail-under=90 --include="app/*"

      - name: Secrets scan
        uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
        with:
          path: ./
          base: ${{ github.event.repository.default_branch }}
          extra_args: --only-verified
Stage 4: Eval Gate

The eval gate is the distinguishing stage of a GenAI pipeline. It runs the model against a frozen golden dataset and asserts that quality and safety metrics meet the configured thresholds. This stage runs in parallel streams to minimise wall-clock time:
Stage 5: Staging Deployment

Staging runs the newly-built image against real (or representative) traffic in shadow mode before any user sees the new responses. Shadow mode means the new model responds in parallel with the current production model; responses are logged but not served to users.

staging:
    needs: [validate, eval-gate]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/myapp \
            app=myregistry/myapp:${{ env.APP_VERSION }} \
            --namespace=staging

      - name: Wait for rollout
        run: kubectl rollout status deployment/myapp --namespace=staging --timeout=5m

      - name: Run integration tests
        run: pytest tests/integration/ --base-url=https://staging.myapp.example.com -q

      - name: Capture latency baseline
        run: |
          python scripts/perf_baseline.py \
            --endpoint https://staging.myapp.example.com/chat \
            --requests 100 \
            --output perf_baseline.json

      - name: Assert latency gate
        run: |
          python -c "
import json, sys
b = json.load(open('perf_baseline.json'))
p95 = b['p95_ms']
prev_p95 = b['prev_p95_ms']
if p95 > prev_p95 * 1.20:
    print(f'Latency regression: p95={p95}ms (+{(p95/prev_p95-1):.0%} vs {prev_p95}ms)')
    sys.exit(1)
print(f'Latency OK: p95={p95}ms (prev={prev_p95}ms)')
"

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with: { name: perf-baseline, path: perf_baseline.json }
Stage 6: Production Deployment with Auto-Rollback

Production deployment uses a blue/green strategy: the new version receives a small percentage of traffic first, monitored live before full cutover. An automated rollback script monitors the safety eval score and reverts if it degrades:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/blue_green_deploy.sh
set -euo pipefail

NEW_VERSION="$1"
NAMESPACE="production"
ROLLBACK_TRIGGER_SCORE="0.97"   # safety score floor

echo "Starting blue/green deploy: $NEW_VERSION"

# 1. Deploy new version alongside current (green)
kubectl set image deployment/myapp-green app="myregistry/myapp:$NEW_VERSION" -n $NAMESPACE
kubectl rollout status deployment/myapp-green -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=3m

# 2. Route 5% of traffic to green
kubectl patch service myapp -n $NAMESPACE \
  -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"track":"green"},"weight":5}}'

# 3. Monitor for 5 minutes
echo "Monitoring for 5 minutes at 5% traffic..."
for i in $(seq 1 5); do
  sleep 60
  SAFETY_SCORE=$(python scripts/live_safety_check.py --namespace $NAMESPACE)
  echo "  Minute $i: safety=$SAFETY_SCORE"
  if (( $(echo "$SAFETY_SCORE < $ROLLBACK_TRIGGER_SCORE" | bc -l) )); then
    echo "Safety score dropped to $SAFETY_SCORE — triggering rollback"
    kubectl patch service myapp -n $NAMESPACE \
      -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"track":"blue"},"weight":100}}'
    exit 1
  fi
done

# 4. Full cutover
echo "Cutting over to 100% green"
kubectl patch service myapp -n $NAMESPACE \
  -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"track":"green"},"weight":100}}'

echo "Deploy complete: $NEW_VERSION at 100% traffic"
Stage 7: Post-Deploy Monitoring and Drift Detection

After deploy, a nightly job re-evaluates the live application against the golden dataset. This catches model drift — silent degradation caused by the LLM provider updating their model without a version change in the API response:

# scripts/nightly_drift_check.py
"""Nightly eval run: detect silent model drift in production."""
import json, datetime, pathlib, sys
from eval.accuracy import run_accuracy
from eval.safety import run_safety

GOLDEN_DATASET = "eval/datasets/golden_v3.jsonl"
DRIFT_LOG = pathlib.Path("eval/drift_history.jsonl")

ACCURACY_FLOOR = 0.85   # below this — alert immediately
SAFETY_FLOOR = 0.99

def load_last_scores() -> dict | None:
    if not DRIFT_LOG.exists():
        return None
    lines = DRIFT_LOG.read_text().strip().splitlines()
    return json.loads(lines[-1]) if lines else None

def run_checks() -> dict:
    accuracy = run_accuracy(GOLDEN_DATASET)
    safety = run_safety(GOLDEN_DATASET)
    return {
        "date": datetime.date.today().isoformat(),
        "accuracy": round(accuracy.pass_rate, 4),
        "safety": round(safety.refusal_accuracy, 4),
    }

def alert(message: str):
    print(f"ALERT: {message}")
    # In production: send to PagerDuty, Slack, etc.

def main():
    current = run_checks()
    last = load_last_scores()

    issues = []
    if current["accuracy"] < ACCURACY_FLOOR:
        issues.append(f"accuracy={current['accuracy']:.1%} below floor {ACCURACY_FLOOR:.1%}")
    if current["safety"] < SAFETY_FLOOR:
        issues.append(f"safety={current['safety']:.1%} below floor {SAFETY_FLOOR:.1%}")

    if last:
        acc_drop = last["accuracy"] - current["accuracy"]
        if acc_drop > 0.05:
            issues.append(f"accuracy dropped {acc_drop:.1%} since {last['date']}")

    for issue in issues:
        alert(issue)

    with DRIFT_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(current) + "\n")

    print(json.dumps(current, indent=2))
    sys.exit(1 if issues else 0)

main()
Version Envelope Pattern

Every artifact produced by the pipeline — the Docker image, eval results, the deploy manifest — carries a version envelope that records the exact combination of code, prompt, and model that was deployed. This enables precise incident attribution and controlled rollback:

# In Kubernetes deployment manifest — CI injects values from CI env:
env:
  - name: APP_VERSION
    value: "1.3.2"
  - name: PROMPT_VERSION
    value: "customer-assistant-v2.4"
  - name: LLM_MODEL
    value: "gpt-4o-2024-11-20"
  - name: EVAL_RUN_ID
    value: "eval-20260702-abc123"
  - name: GIT_SHA
    value: "4cc5fce6"
What Conventional CI/CD Pipelines Lack
The table below summarises the GenAI-specific additions to a standard pipeline and why each is necessary:
Stage
GenAI addition
Why it's needed
Source
Prompt and model version pinned in repo
LLM behaviour changes with prompt and model; both must be versioned
Build
Version envelope in image labels
Correlates deployed behaviour with the exact eval run that approved it
Validate
Mutation score gate on eval harness
Ensures the eval infrastructure itself is not broken
Eval
LLM-as-judge accuracy and safety checks
No deterministic oracle exists for natural language output
Staging
Shadow mode + latency baseline
LLM latency is high and variable; baseline comparison catches regressions
Production
Live safety score monitoring + auto-rollback
Model provider updates can silently change behaviour post-deploy
Monitor
Nightly drift evaluation
Model drift occurs without any code change — only ongoing eval catches it
Summary

A GenAI application delivery pipeline extends conventional CI/CD with four key capabilities: eval gates that judge non-deterministic output, prompt versioning woven into the version envelope, shadow-mode staging that captures live-traffic latency without user risk, and post-deploy nightly evaluation that detects model drift before it affects users.
Use path filters to trigger the expensive eval gate only when prompts, eval harness, or application code changes.
Embed the version envelope (app version + prompt version + model + eval run ID + git SHA) in every artifact.
Run accuracy, safety, and mutation score checks in parallel in the eval gate to minimise wall-clock time.
Deploy via blue/green with live safety score monitoring and an automated rollback if the score drops.
Run a nightly drift check against the golden eval dataset; alert when scores drop below the configured floor.
